title: EXPOSÉ XIII BASE CHANGE THEOREM FOR A PROPER MORPHISM: END OF THE PROOF

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XIII BASE CHANGE THEOREM FOR A PROPER MORPHISM: END OF THE PROOF

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XIII
BASE CHANGE THEOREM FOR A PROPER MORPHISM: END OF THE PROOFby M. Artin
Contents

1. The projective and flat case
2. The case of relative dimension \(\leq 1\)
3. An auxiliary result on the Picard group
1. The projective and flat case
Let us recall the statement of XII 5.9 bis in the case considered here:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a Noetherian Henselian ring, and \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a projective and flat morphism. Let \(X_0\) be the closed fiber of \(X/S\). The restriction functor \[ \operatorname{Et}(X) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Et}(X_0) \] is an equivalence of categories.

As we have already remarked, it follows from XII 6.5 (i) that the arrow is fully faithful. It remains to prove that each étale covering \(Y_0\) of \(X_0\) is induced by an étale covering \(Y\) of \(X\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2

Let \(S\) be locally Noetherian, \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) projective and flat, and \(Y \subset X\) a subscheme. The subfunctor \(\varepsilon = \prod_{X/S} Y/X : (\mathrm{Sch})/S \longrightarrow (\mathrm{Ens})\) of the final functor "which expresses the condition \(Y = X\)", i.e. such that for \(S' \to S\), one has \[ \varepsilon(S') = \begin{cases} \varnothing & \text{if } Y' \neq X' \\ \{\varnothing\} & \text{if } Y' = X' \end{cases} \] (where a prime denotes the effect of the base change \(S' \longrightarrow S\)) is representable by a subprescheme \(Z\) of \(S\). If \(Y\) is open (resp. closed), so is \(Z\).

Proof. Let \(Y\) be closed in the open set \(U\) of \(X\), and let \(C\) be the closed complement of \(U\). Since \(f\) is proper, the image of \(C\) in \(S\) is a closed set \(D\), and it is clear that the condition \(U = X\) is represented by the open set \(S - D\) of \(S\). We may therefore suppose that we already have \(U = X\), i.e. that \(Y\) is a closed subscheme. Consider the surjective morphism \(\mathcal{O}_X \xrightarrow{\alpha} \mathcal{O}_Y\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; to say that \(Y = X\) amounts to the same as saying that there exists a morphism \(\mathcal{O}_Y \xrightarrow{\beta} \mathcal{O}_X\) such that the composite \(\beta\alpha\) is the identity, that is, that there exists a section of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathcal{O}_Y, \mathcal{O}_X)\) which lifts the identity section of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{O}_X)\). Now it follows from EGA III 7.7.8 that the functor which to \(S'/S\) associates the set of sections of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathcal{O}_Y, \mathcal{O}_X)\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)) is represented by a vector bundle \(V_1\) (resp. \(V_2\)) over \(S\). Let \(\varphi : V_1 \longrightarrow V_2\) be the morphism \(u \mapsto u\alpha\) induced by \(\mathcal{O}_X \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_Y\), and \(s : S \longrightarrow V_2\) the section corresponding to the identity \(\mathcal{O}_X \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_X\). The inverse image of the section \(s\) by \(\varphi\) is identified with a closed subscheme \(Z\) of \(S\), and it is clear that \(Z\) represents the functor \(\varepsilon\), whence the lemma.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a projective and flat morphism, and \(E\) a locally free sheaf on \(X\). Let \[ QE : (\mathrm{Sch})/S \longrightarrow (\mathrm{Ens}) \] be the functor which to \(S'/S\) associates the set \(QE(S')\) of quotient sheaves \(A'\) of \(E' = E \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathcal{O}_{S'}\), equipped with a structure of étale Algebra over \(\mathcal{O}_{X'}\). This functor is representable by a scheme locally of finite type over \(S\).

Proof. The functor \(\operatorname{Quot}(E)\), \(\operatorname{Quot}(E)(S') = \) the set of quotients of \(E'\) flat over \(S'\), is representable by a scheme locally of finite type (TDTE IV, exp. 221), say by \(Z\,/\,S\). Let \(M_u\) be the "universal" quotient of \(E \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathcal{O}_Z\) over \(X_Z\). The subfunctor of \(\operatorname{Quot}(E)\) of quotients which are flat over \(X'\) is represented by an open set \(U\) of \(Z\). Indeed, let \(M'\) be a quotient of \(E'\), given by an \(S\)-morphism \(S' \longrightarrow Z\). Since \(M'\) is flat over \(S'\), to say that it is flat over \(X'\) amounts to the same as saying that for each point \(s' \in S'\), the module \(M'_{s'}\) is flat over the fiber \(X'_{s'}\) (EGA IV 5.9), which is equivalent to \((M_u)_z\) being flat over the fiber \(Z_z\) at the point \(z\) image of \(s'\). Now the set \(U\) of \(z \in Z\) such that \((M_u)_z\) is flat over \(Z_z\), i.e. such that \(M_u\) is flat over \(Z\) at the points of \(Z_s\), is open in \(Z\) by virtue of EGA IV 11.1.5, whence the assertion.
It now suffices to represent the relative functor \(QE/U[f]\). We are thus reduced to the following lemma, replacing \(S\) by \(U\) and \(X\) by \(X \times_S U\):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a projective and flat morphism, and \(M\) a locally free sheaf on \(\mathcal{O}_X\). The functor \(\mathrm{Alg\acute{e}t} : (\mathrm{Sch})/S \longrightarrow (\mathrm{Ens})\), which to \(S'/S\) associates the set \(\mathrm{Alg\acute{e}t}(S')\) of algebra structures on \(M'\) étale over \(\mathcal{O}_{X'}\), is representable by a scheme of finite type over \(S\).

Proof. We shall say, for brevity, "representable", meaning implicitly "by a relative scheme of finite type". The vector bundles encountered will all be defined by coherent sheaves, hence will be of finite type. A bilinear composition law on \(M'\) is given by a section of the locally free sheaf \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(M' \otimes M', M')\). Since \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(M' \otimes M', M')\) is \(S\)-flat, it follows from EGA III 7.7.6 that the functor "bilinear composition law" is represented by a vector bundle \(Z\) over \(S\). One must verify that the additional conditions on the law of being associative, commutative, and of having a unit section, are represented by a subscheme of \(Z\), and one may (by considering the relative functor) replace \(S\) by \(Z\), and hence may suppose that one has a composition law given on \(M\), and show that one can represent the subfunctor of \(S\) expressing the preceding conditions by a subscheme of \(S\).

Now for associativity, the condition is that two sections of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(M \otimes M \otimes M, M)\) become equal under base change \(S' \longrightarrow S\). Let \(Y\) be the fiber product of these sections over the vector bundle corresponding to \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(M \otimes M \otimes M, M)\). The scheme \(Y\) is identified with a closed subscheme of \(X\), and associativity after \(S' \longrightarrow S\) is expressed by the condition \(Y' = X'\). It therefore defines a subfunctor of \(S\) representable by a closed subscheme of \(S\) according to 1.2. For commutativity the condition is that two sections of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(M \otimes M, M)\) become equal, which again defines a subfunctor of \(S\) representable by a closed subscheme according to 1.2. Suppose finally, which is now permissible, that the law is already associative and commutative, and let us examine the condition for the existence of a unit section, which will then be unique. The functor whose value at \(S'/S\) is the set of sections of \(M'\) is representable by a vector bundle, according to EGA III 7.7.6, and everything reduces to representing the subfunctor of the latter corresponding to unit sections (taking into account the uniqueness just recalled). Relativizing as usual, one may suppose that a section \(\varepsilon\) of \(M\) is already given, and show that the subfunctor of \(S\) expressing that it becomes a unit section is representable. Now, the condition that \(\varepsilon\) be a unit is that the composite of the obvious morphisms \(M' \longrightarrow M' \otimes M' \longrightarrow M'\) be the identity, that is, that two determined sections of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(M, M)\) become equal. One concludes again thanks to 1.2.

Suppose finally that a unitary commutative algebra structure is given on the locally free module \(M\). The condition of being étale is open in \(X\) (SGA I 4.5), hence representable over \(S\) by an (open) subscheme. This completes the proof of 1.4, and hence of 1.3.

1.5. Let now \(Z/S\) be the object which represents the functor considered in 1.3, and let us examine the condition on \(Z\) of being smooth over \(S\) at a point \(z\). Translating SGA 1 III 3.1 in terms of the functor \(QE\), one finds the following condition:

Let \(B\) be a local Artinian ring, \(\mathfrak{M} = \mathfrak{r}B\), \(J\) an ideal of \(B\) such that \(J\mathfrak{M} = 0\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec} B\), \(S'' = \operatorname{Spec} B/J\) and \(S' \longrightarrow S\) a morphism. One uses obvious notation for the effects of the base changes \(S' \longrightarrow S\) and \(S'' \longrightarrow S\).

(∗) For each quotient \(A''\) of \(E''\) equipped with a structure of étale algebra over \(\mathcal{O}_{X''}\), corresponding to an \(S\)-morphism \(S'' \longrightarrow Z\) with image \(z\), there exists a quotient \(A'\) of \(E'\), equipped with a structure of étale algebra over \(\mathcal{O}_{X'}\), which induces it.
Now since \(X'\) and \(X''\) have the same underlying space, an algebra \(A'\) which induces \(A''\) is already determined up to unique isomorphism (SGA I 8.3).
One may therefore express the condition (∗∗) as follows:
(∗∗) Let \(A'\) be an étale algebra over \(\mathcal{O}_{X'}\), and \(A''\) the algebra induced from \(A'\) on \(X''\).

Each surjective homomorphism \[ E'' \xrightarrow{\varphi''} A'' \] corresponding to a morphism \(S'' \longrightarrow Z\) with image \(z\), lifts to a homomorphism (necessarily surjective by virtue of Nakayama's lemma) \[ E' \xrightarrow{\varphi'} A' \]

From the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow B/J \longrightarrow 0 \] one deduces an exact sequence of sheaves on \(X'\) : \[ 0 \longrightarrow J \otimes \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E', A') \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E', A') \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E'', A'') \longrightarrow 0 \ . \]

The obstruction to lifting \(\varphi''\) lies in \(H^1(X', J \otimes \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E', A'))\). Let \(S_0 = \operatorname{Spec} B/\mathfrak{M}\). Since \(J\mathfrak{M} = 0\), one has \[ J \otimes \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E', A') \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} J \otimes \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E_0, A_0) \ , \] and it is clear that \[ H^1(X', J \otimes \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E_0, A_0)) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} J \otimes H^1(X_0, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E_0, A_0)) \ . \]
Taking into account the fact that cohomology commutes with extension of the base field, one finds the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.6

Let \(Z/S\) be the scheme which represents the functor of 1.3, \(X_Z = X \times_S Z\), and \(A_Z\) the quotient étale Algebra ("general") of \(E_Z\). Then \(Z/S\) is smooth at every point \(z_0\) such that \[ H^1(X_0, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E_0, A_0)) = 0 \ , \] where the index zero denotes the restriction to the fiber \(X_0\) of \(X_Z / Z\) at the point \(z_0\).

We can now complete the proof of theorem 1.1: with the notation of the theorem, let \(Y_0/X_0\) be an étale covering, given by a sheaf of Algebras \(A_0\), étale over \(\mathcal{O}_{X_0}\). For \(n\) large enough, \(A_0(n)\) is generated by its sections (EGA III 2.2.1). One thus has a surjection \[ \mathcal{O}_{X_0}^N \longrightarrow A_0(n) \longrightarrow 0 \ , \] whence a surjection \[ \mathcal{O}_{X_0}^N(-n) = E_0 \longrightarrow A_0 \longrightarrow 0 \ . \]

Let us choose \(n\) large enough that one also has \[ (+) \qquad H^1(X_0, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E_0, A_0)) = 0 \ , \] which is possible by virtue of (EGA III 2.2.1), since \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E_0, A_0) = [\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathcal{O}_{X_0}^N, A_0)](n) = [A_0(n)]^N \ . \]

Set \(E = \mathcal{O}_X^N(-n)\), and let \(Z/S\) be the scheme which represents the functor considered in 1.3. The quotient \(A_0\) of \(E_0\) corresponds to a point \(z_0 \in Z\) of the closed fiber of \(Z/S\), rational over \(k(s)\), and according to 1.5 and \((+)\), \(Z/S\) is smooth at the point \(z_0\). Now since \(S\) is the spectrum of a Henselian ring and \(Z/S\) is smooth at the point \(z_0\) of the closed fiber, one can find a section of \(Z/S\) passing through \(z_0\): this is for example immediate from the original definition (SGA II 1.1) of smoothness. It follows that \(A_0\) is induced by an Algebra \(A\) étale over \(X\) (quotient of \(E\)), which completes the proof of 1.1.
2. The case of relative dimension \(\leq 1\)
The assertion is the following:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1

With the notation of XII 5.9 bis, the functor \(\operatorname{Et}(X) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Et}(X_0)\) is an equivalence of categories if \(S\) is Noetherian strictly local, and \(f\) is projective and of dimension \(\leq 1\).
According to XII 8.5, this will complete the proof of XII 5.1 (ii), which generalizes both 1.1 and 2.1.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a local Noetherian ring \(A\), and let \(X/S\) be projective, of relative dimension \(\leq n\). There exists a projective and flat \(S\)-scheme \(Z/S\) of relative dimension \(\leq n\) and a closed immersion \(X \hookrightarrow Z\) over \(S\).

Proof. Let \(X \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_S\) be a projective embedding over \(S\), and let \(J \subset A[x_0, \ldots, x_N]\) be the homogeneous ideal which defines \(X\). Let \(k = A/\mathfrak{r}A\), \(S_0 = \operatorname{Spec} k\), \(X_0 = X \otimes_S k\). Then \(X_0\) is defined by the homogeneous ideal \(J_0 = \operatorname{Im}(J \longrightarrow k[x_0, \ldots, x_N])\). Since \(\dim X_0 \leq n\), it is well known that there exist elements \(f^0_1, \ldots, f^0_{N-n}\) of \(J_0\) which define a subscheme \(Z_0\) of \(\mathbb{P}^N_k\) of dimension \(n\). Let \(f_i\) \((1 \leq i \leq N-n)\) be elements of \(J\) which lift the \(f^0_i\), and let \(Z\) be the subscheme of \(\mathbb{P}^N_S\) defined by \(f_1, \ldots, f_\nu\). By virtue of EGA IV 11.3, \(Z\) is flat over \(S\); on the other hand, by virtue of EGA IV 13.1.5 it is of relative dimension \(\dim Z_0 = n\). This completes the proof of 2.2.

Proof of 2.1. Let \(X/S\) be projective of relative dimension \(\leq 1\), with \(S\) Noetherian strictly local. We may suppose \(X\) (hence \(X_0\)) connected. Let \(X \longrightarrow Z\) be a closed immersion over \(S\) with \(Z\) projective, flat and of dimension \(\leq 1\), cf. 2.2. Let \(s_0\) be the closed point of \(S\). One has a corresponding diagram of schemes \[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Et}(Z) \ar[r] \ar[d]_a & \operatorname{Et}(X) \ar[d]^b \\ \operatorname{Et}(Z_0) \ar[r]^c & \operatorname{Et}(X_0) } \]

We wish to prove that \(b\) is an equivalence. According to XII 5.8, \(b\) is fully faithful, and it remains to prove that it is essentially surjective. Now \(a\) is essentially surjective according to 1.1, and it thus suffices to prove that \(c\) is essentially surjective. We are thus reduced (dropping the \({}_0\)) to the following proposition:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3

Let \(k\) be a separably closed field, \(Z\) a \(k\)-scheme, locally of finite type and of dimension \(\leq 1\), and \(X \hookrightarrow Z\) a connected closed subscheme. Then every étale covering of \(X\) is induced by an étale covering of \(Z\).

Proof. One may suppose \(Z\) and \(X\) of dimension 1, the case where \(X\) is of dimension zero being trivial thanks to the hypothesis made on \(k\). Then \(Z = X \cup Y\), where \(Y\) is the closed subscheme closure of \(Z - X\). Let \(V = X \cap Y\) be the intersection scheme, which is a discrete scheme, i.e. locally Artinian. The morphism \(X \amalg Y \longrightarrow Z\) is finite and surjective, hence a strict descent morphism for the category of étale coverings (SGA IX 4.7). One evidently has \[ (\ast) \qquad (X \amalg Y) \times_Z (X \amalg Y) = X \amalg Y \amalg V^{(1)} \amalg V^{(2)} \qquad (V^{(i)} = V) \ , \] where \(\operatorname{pr}_i\) sends \(V^{(i)}\) into the component \(X\) of \(X \amalg Y\) and \(V^{(j)}\) into the component \(Y\) for \(j \neq i\).

Let \(X'/X\) be an étale covering of degree \(n\), and let \(Y''/Y\) be a covering of degree \(n\) (for example the trivial covering of degree \(n\)), so that \(X' \amalg Y''\) is a covering of \(X \amalg Y\) of degree \(n\). It suffices to find descent data for this covering and for the morphism \(X \amalg Y \longrightarrow Z\). Looking at \((\ast)\), one sees that a descent datum for this morphism amounts simply to an isomorphism \[ X' \times_X V = V' \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} V'' = Y'' \times_Y V \ . \] Since \(k\) is separably closed, so is \(k(z_i)\), and consequently each étale covering of \(V\) is completely decomposed, whence \(V' \simeq V''\), which gives the result.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.4

One can avoid recourse to the delicate result of SGA IX and to descent theory, by a direct argument, showing that the category of étale coverings of \(Z\) is equivalent to the category of triples \((X', Y', \theta)\), where \(X'(Y')\) is an étale covering of \(X(Y)\) and \(\theta\) a \(V\)-isomorphism \(X' \times_X V \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} Y' \times_Y V\). This result, valid for any scheme \(Z\) union of two closed subschemes \(X, Y\), is established directly by means of the analogous result for the determination of quasi-coherent Modules on \(X\) (when \(X = V(\mathfrak{J})\), \(Y = V(\mathfrak{K})\), \(\mathfrak{J} \cap \mathfrak{K} = 0\)).

pages.title: 3. An auxiliary result on the Picard group(∗)

blocks.html: 3. An auxiliary result on the Picard group(∗)

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian scheme, \(X_0\) a closed subscheme of \(X\) such that a) \(\dim X_0 \leq 1\), and b) for every non-empty connected closed part \(Y\) of \(X\), \(Y_0 = Y \cap X_0\) is non-empty connected.

(i) Let \(Z_0\) be the union of the \(\overline{\{x\}} \cap X_0\) for \(x \in \operatorname{Ass} \mathcal{O}_X\) such that \(\overline{\{x\}} \cap X_0\) is reduced to a point. Then for every Cartier divisor \(D_0\) on \(X_0\) such that \(\operatorname{supp}(D_0)\) does not meet \(Z_0\), there exists a Cartier divisor \(D\) on \(X\), inducing \(D_0\) (and if \(D_0 \geq 0\), one can take \(D \geq 0\)).

(ii) Suppose that there exists an affine open set \(U_0\) of \(X_0\) containing the finite set \(\operatorname{Ass} \mathcal{O}_{X_0} \cup Z_0\) (a condition automatically satisfied if \(X_0\) is Noetherian and if \(X_0\) admits an ample invertible sheaf). Then the homomorphism \(\operatorname{Pic}(X) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Pic}(X_0)\) is surjective.

Proof. (i) Since \(\dim X_0 \leq 1\), one sees at once that every Cartier divisor \(D_0\) on \(X_0\) has discrete support, hence is the difference of two positive Cartier divisors, with supports contained in that of \(D_0\). This reduces us in (i) to the case where \(D_0\) is positive, hence where for every \(x \in \operatorname{Supp} D_0\), \(D_0\) is defined at \(x\) by a non-zero-divisor \(f_x \in \operatorname{rad}(\mathcal{O}_{X_0, x})\). Then \(f_x\) is induced by a section \(g_x\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) on an open neighborhood \(U_x\) of \(x\). I claim that on taking \(U_x\) small enough, \(g_x\) is a non-zero-divisor in \(\mathcal{O}_{U_x}\), i.e., \(V(g_x) \cap \operatorname{Ass} \mathcal{O}_{U_x} = \varnothing\). Indeed, it follows from a) and b) that for every \(x \in X\), and in particular for \(z \in \operatorname{Ass} \mathcal{O}_X\), \(\bar{z} \cap X_0\) is a connected closed set of \(X_0\) hence is either reduced to a single closed point, or to a connected union of irreducible components of dimension 1 on \(X_0\). Since \(x \notin Z_0\) it follows that in the first case one has \(x \notin \bar{z}\). But if \(z \in \operatorname{Ass} \mathcal{O}_X\) of the second type, one cannot have \(x \in \bar{z}\) and \(z \in V(g_x)\), since then \(g_x\) hence \(f_x\) would vanish on one of the irreducible components of \(\bar{z} \cap X_0\), which is also an irreducible component of \(X_0\), which is incompatible with the fact that \(f_x\) is a non-zero-divisor in \(\mathcal{O}_{X_0, x}\). Therefore, up to shrinking \(U_x\), one may suppose that \(V(g_x) \cap \operatorname{Ass} \mathcal{O}_{U_x} = \varnothing\), i.e. \(g_x\) is a non-zero-divisor. Let \(Y_x\) be the closure of \(V(g_x)\) in \(X\); then \(Y_x \cap X_0 \cap U_x \subset V(g_x) \cap X_0\) admits \(x\) as an isolated point, hence so does \(Y_x \cap X_0\), hence by condition b) of the proposition, \(Y_x\) admits a connected component \(Y'_x\) such that \(Y'_x \cap X_0 = \{x\}\). Moreover, one has \(Y'_x \subset U_x\), for \(T_x = Y'_x - Y'_x \cap U_x\) is a closed set of \(X\) such that \(T_x \cap X_0 = \varnothing\), hence \(T_x = \varnothing\) by virtue of condition b). We now take the Cartier divisor \(D'_x\) on \(X\) which induces \(Y'_x\) on \(U_x\), and induces zero on \(X - Y'_x\), and shall set \(D = \sum_x D'_x\), which is a positive Cartier divisor on \(X\), evidently inducing \(D_0\) on \(X_0\).

(ii) The conclusion follows from (i) and from the fact that every invertible module \(\underline{L}_0\) on \(X_0\) is isomorphic to the module defined by a Cartier divisor \(D_0\) whose support does not meet \(Z_0\). This last fact means indeed that \(\underline{L}_0\) admits a section defined on an open set \(V_0\) containing \(\operatorname{Ass} \mathcal{O}_{X_0} \cup Z_0\), this section vanishing at no point of the latter set. Now it suffices to prove this on replacing \(X_0\) by the affine open set \(U_0\), and in this case it is well known and comes from the fact that an invertible module on a semi-local scheme is isomorphic to the structure sheaf. Finally, it is well known that if \(X_0\) admits an ample sheaf, then every finite part of \(X_0\) is contained in an affine open set. This proves (ii).
One has the following corollary, which generalizes XII 8.6 and completes the proof of 5.1 (iii):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a Noetherian Henselian ring, and \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a proper morphism of relative dimension \(\leq 1\). Then for every closed subscheme \(X'_0\) of \(X\) having the same underlying space as the closed fiber \(X_0\) of \(X/S\), the canonical map \(\operatorname{Pic}(X) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Pic}(X'_0)\) is surjective.

Indeed, one applies the proposition to \((X, X'_0)\), noting that condition a) is satisfied by hypothesis, and b) by virtue of XII 5.7. It remains to verify the hypothesis made in ii) of the existence of a \(U_0\), which follows from the fact that \(X'_0\) is necessarily projective (like any proper curve over a field).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

The reader who does not wish to admit or reconstruct the fact that proper curves over a field are projective will confine himself to using the corollary while supposing already that \(f\) is projective, which suffices for the application we had in view here. Let us point out on the other hand that, admitting the projectivity of proper algebraic curves over a field, one concludes that under the conditions of 3.2 \(X\) is necessarily projective over \(S\), as one sees by choosing an ample \(\xi_0 \in \operatorname{Pic}(X_0)\) and lifting it to a \(\xi \in \operatorname{Pic}(X)\), and applying EGA III 4.7.1.

footnotes.html: (∗) It also appears in EGA IV 21.9.11, 21.9.12 in a slightly more general form; in particular it suffices in 3.2 that \(f\) be separated instead of proper.

blocks.html: Bibliography

[TDTE] GROTHENDIECK, A. Technique de descente et théorèmes d'existence en Géométrie Algébrique, in Fondements de la Géométrie Algébrique (Extraits du Sém. Bourbaki 1957-62) Secrétariat mathématique, 11, rue Pierre Curie.
